Back: Ensure members understand which incidents require a formal Complaint Report and which are properly documented through alternative means. Misdocumentation can affect crime statistics and case tracking.
Supervisors must ensure that complaints taken for another command are promptly and accurately forwarded. Delays can compromise investigations.

Patrol supervisors should ensure that officers conducting follow-up canvasses or receiving new information properly route it to the assigned investigative unit rather than creating new complaint reports.
Supervisors are responsible for ensuring the complaint file is maintained in an orderly and up-to-date fashion, facilitating case review and audits.
The Intelligence Division consultation for public officials is a new emphasis in the 2025 revision. This is a "shall" requirement, not discretionary.

The patrol supervisor has dual responsibilities: (1) promptly report to SVU, and (2) safeguard the crime scene pending detective arrival. The supervisor "shall" ensure evidence preservation -- this is mandatory.
These notifications frequently involve elderly or incapacitated persons. Ensure responding members are sensitive to the circumstances and thorough in documentation.

A DAT-eligible prisoner should NEVER be held solely for debriefing purposes. The only exception is when there is reasonable suspicion that the prisoner is involved in another crime. Holding a DAT-eligible prisoner without legal basis exposes the Department to liability.

The three categories are frequently tested. Remember: Category 1 = who the defendant IS, Category 2 = what the defendant DID, Category 3 = what the defendant MIGHT DO (future danger requiring court protection).
Supervisors must track DAT conferrals to ensure they happen within the required timeframe. A missed conferral can result in case dismissal.
Strangulation injuries are often internal and not immediately visible. Supervisors must ensure victims receive medical evaluation even when external injuries are absent.

The Training Sergeant (not the DV Sergeant) is responsible for ensuring all UMOS know how to take and upload DV photos. The DVPO reviews daily to ensure photos were taken. Injuries that were not visible initially may become visible later — follow-up photography is critical.

Petty offenses have the most restrictive requirements — the offense must be in the officer's presence, within the geographical area of employment, AND within the county of occurrence or adjoining county. Crimes (felonies and misdemeanors) require only reasonable cause and have no county limitation within the geographical area.
The supervisor's verification at the scene BEFORE removal is a critical checkpoint. The supervisor must also ensure a member is designated to document all MOS present and BWC activation status.
The desk officer must personally observe the physical and mental condition of ALL prisoners entering the command. High-risk prisoners require TWO MOS escort at ALL times — not just during transport.
Ensure arresting officers follow identification procedures thoroughly. Misidentification can lead to wrongful release or processing errors.
Supervisors must monitor felony processing timelines closely to ensure arraignment deadlines are met.
Supervisors must verify the validity of the warrant or wanted status before authorizing transfer of custody.

Supervisors must ensure all required notifications are made promptly. Failure to notify specialized units can compromise investigations and inter-agency relationships. The Intelligence Division notification list is particularly important — UN employees, foreign armed forces, and criminal anarchy all require immediate Intelligence Division contact.

The desk officer's authority over religious head covering decisions is absolute at the command level. This mirrors the desk officer's broad authority over prisoner processing decisions within the command.

Supervisors must ensure that strip searches are NEVER conducted routinely. Every strip search requires (1) reasonable suspicion, (2) immediate supervisor or borough court section supervisor approval, and (3) same-gender officer in a private, secure location. Document the basis for reasonable suspicion. NEVER allow a body cavity search under any circumstances.

The supervisor must verify that the civilian's arrest meets the legal standard — the offense must have been committed in the civilian's presence. If the legal basis is questionable, consult with Legal Bureau.

Supervisors must determine the citizenship status of all prisoners and ensure proper consular notification. Failure to provide mandatory notification can have diplomatic consequences and may compromise prosecution.
This mirrors the search guidelines in P.G. 208-05. The desk officer has ultimate authority within the command on religious head covering decisions during arrest processing.
Supervisors should ensure that eligible defendants are identified for Project Reset during processing. The program criteria are narrow — age 18+, first-time offender, non-violent misdemeanor only.

Supervisors play a critical role in MOS DV incidents. The C.O./Duty Captain makes the duty status decision (not IAB), but must have IAB conduct a background check first. The confidential file requirement is ongoing — the C.O. maintains it for ALL MOS who have been involved in DV incidents.

Supervisors must verify that officers correctly distinguish between violations (summons-eligible for 16-year-olds) and non-VTL misdemeanors (not summons-eligible for 16-year-olds). This is a common error in the field.

When a Criminal Court summons is issued under Code 3 (law enforcement exception), a patrol supervisor MUST respond to the scene to verify the exception. This is a mandatory supervisory response — failure to respond is a procedural violation. Ensure officer details reason in digital Activity Log.
Supervisors should ensure officers correctly route OATH-eligible offenses to OATH rather than Criminal Court. Misrouting creates unnecessary court appearances and administrative burden.
Supervisors should periodically review parking summons issuance to verify all 5 mandatory elements are present. A pattern of dismissals due to missing elements indicates a training deficiency.

The desk officer is responsible for tracking unclaimed keys and ensuring timely invoicing to the Property Clerk after 48 hours. Supervisors should include ignition key removals in their review of daily activity.
Supervisors should monitor that officers conduct mandatory license checks at every qualifying stop. The Activity Log entry when DMV is inoperative creates the audit trail.

Verify that officers park the violator's vehicle in a legal area rather than leaving it in traffic. The vehicle remains until the registered owner (not the violator with the suspended license) arranges removal.

Supervisors must review Summons Plea Forms for accuracy and ensure they are forwarded within the 30-day deadline. Verify that officers claiming "necessary police action" can articulate how the action enhanced safety — "convenience" is explicitly excluded.

Supervisors play a critical role in the summons voiding process. Every voided summons requires supervisory review to prevent abuse. Monitor voiding patterns — an officer with an unusually high void rate may indicate a training issue or integrity concern.
Supervisors must verify that Vehicle Reports are completed for every stop. Missing reports indicate either unreported stops or incomplete documentation — both require corrective action.

The desk officer is the central authority for prisoner management at the command level. Sergeants must ensure desk officers are actively managing cellblock assignments, custody records, and attendant assignments — not delegating these responsibilities.

Before ANY prisoner is removed from the command, the desk officer must step from behind the desk and personally check that prisoners are properly secured. This is a physical, hands-on duty — not a visual check from behind the desk.

The immediate supervisor (sergeant) makes the determination on whether to use less restrictive restraints for pregnant prisoners. This is a direct supervisory decision — not a desk officer or commanding officer decision. Document the assessment and rationale.

The desk officer must actively track whether the arresting agency has provided relief guards. If no relief arrives within 1 hour, a follow-up notification is required. Do not allow NYPD resources to be used as indefinite guard replacements for other agencies.

The patrol supervisor must respond to ALL unusual occurrences involving prisoners. Document the incident thoroughly in the Command Log, prepare required reports, and notify the commanding officer for serious incidents (death, escape, serious injury).

Sergeants have a dual responsibility: (1) ensure the designated MOS is conducting 30-minute inspections, and (2) personally and periodically inspect prisoners, recording their condition (sleeping, alert) and physical position (sitting, lying down). This documentation can be critical in liability situations.

Menstrual hygiene products must be provided upon request — do not wait for the prisoner to ask repeatedly. A female MOS must be assigned to escort the prisoner to the restroom. Failure to provide these basic accommodations is a supervisory failure.

When a prisoner requires medical treatment, the patrol supervisor must be notified. The sergeant should verify that the desk officer has properly documented the condition, arranged transport, and assigned a guard. Prisoners with medical conditions cannot simply be held in the cellblock. The supervisor must also evaluate the need for additional personnel, ESU, and ensure leg restraints are applied for hospital transport.

If a hospitalized prisoner is discharged before arraignment, the guarding member must obtain a signed medical release and escort the prisoner to court in rear cuffs. The sergeant should verify this process is followed — prisoners should not be released from the hospital without proper documentation and escort.

The desk officer has authority to authorize additional meals beyond the standard three periods. This includes situations where a prisoner missed a scheduled meal due to processing activities. Sergeants should ensure meal requests are handled promptly — denying food to a prisoner who missed a meal period is not acceptable.

For unusual occurrences, the CO/Duty Captain must personally investigate and prepare typed letterhead reports. The distribution is extensive — 3 copies DIRECT to Chief of Dept, plus 7 additional individual copies. For escapes, IAB must be notified in addition to Operations Unit.

The patrol supervisor is the first-line authority for consent to release a prisoner. This conferral is mandatory — the arresting officer cannot unilaterally void an arrest or release a prisoner. If the patrol supervisor is unavailable, the desk officer or BCS supervisor serves as the alternate authority.

When a voided arrest involves a fingerprinted prisoner, the BCS intake supervisor must fax DCJS to seal the record. Sergeants should verify this step is completed — failure to seal creates a permanent record from an invalid arrest.

When there is a disagreement between the desk officer or BCS supervisor and an arresting officer from another agency regarding prisoner release, the matter is escalated to the precinct commander or duty captain. Sergeants should not attempt to resolve inter-agency disputes at their level — escalate promptly.

The BCS Supervisor is the key decision-maker for Special Category designation, working in conjunction with EMS. The sergeant must ensure all 8 handling steps are completed, especially the MTP preparation and the "Recommend Prisoner Be Separated" checkbox.

The Patrol Supervisor ensures the A/O-E/O accompanies the arrestee during transport (step 8). The desk officer is responsible for arranging both transports and conferring with BCS. The van operator must be qualified and trained in wheelchair lift operation.
The roll call clerk shall access the CPR System 3 times daily:
The desk officer shall:
The roll call clerk shall:
The ACU supervisor shall notify the CO in writing when:
The supervisor determines if conferral with a Department attorney is necessary. This is a judgment call that falls directly on the sergeant.
A Command Log entry is required with the following information:

Sergeants must understand that they cannot override Department attorney advice. If a sergeant believes the attorney's advice is incorrect, the proper course is to notify the Department attorney of the conflicting opinion — not to direct the member to disregard the attorney's guidance. Following this protocol protects both the member and the supervisor from disciplinary action and civil liability.

Sergeants must ensure that civil lawsuit papers are handled promptly — failure to forward the REQUEST FOR LEGAL ASSISTANCE in time can result in a default judgment against the member. The CO must check both IAB Records Unit and Department Advocate's Office before completing Part B. The WHITE copy goes DIRECT to Legal Bureau, not through channels. Follow up after 10 business days.
Supervisors should ensure members understand the mandatory nature of cooperation with the AG's Misconduct Investigative Office. There is no discretion here — full cooperation is required.
When a member's court appearance is scheduled on an RDO:
Sergeants should be aware that the Integrity Control Officer monitors RDO court appearances for patterns that may indicate integrity issues.

Supervisors must ensure members understand that unilateral cancellation attempts do not relieve them of the obligation to appear. Only explicit concurrence from the requesting agency cancels the appearance.

Supervisors must understand that ANY Department document relevant to a case is considered to be in the prosecutor's possession. Failure to produce it is a Brady violation that can overturn a conviction.
For summary arrests, the arresting/assigned officer shall:
Have the member logged into the computer system IMMEDIATELY.
This is a direct sergeant responsibility and a likely exam topic.

Codes 5 (Officer No-Show), 6 (No Prima Facie case), 7 (Cannot identify motorist), and 8 (No recollection) are the codes most likely to trigger training sergeant review and corrective action. These reflect officer performance issues.

Supervisors must ensure officers understand the guardrail boundaries, do not participate in the election process, and properly handle disputes about voting rights by referring to the county Supreme Court.
Ensure all election materials are accounted for and properly secured. Verify that the transition from active voting to post-closing procedures occurs smoothly and without interference.
Sergeants are the preferred rank for conducting rifle/shotgun permit surrender investigations.
When a subordinate MOS becomes the subject of an ERPO, the supervisor must ensure immediate CO notification and initiation of the Modified Assignment process.

Patrol supervisors shall inspect Activity Logs to ensure NYCHA facilities are being inspected by members on post. Supervisors should recommend Departmental Recognition for burglary arrests at NYCHA facilities.
Patrol supervisor should respond to ensure proper coordination between NYPD and NYCHA maintenance staff, particularly when occupants include elderly or medically vulnerable individuals.
Supervisors shall review Activity Logs to verify interior patrols are being conducted and documented. Ensure members are varying patrol times and routes to maximize deterrent effect.

Supervisors must ensure that communication accommodations are provided promptly and that the individual's preferred method of communication is the primary consideration. For pre-planned events, arrange interpreter services in advance through the Chief of Personnel.
Patrol supervisor must investigate immediately when a press member is assaulted or harassed. Notification to DCPI must be made without delay. DCPI is available via phone 24/7.

Supervisors must ensure members understand that press access cannot be restricted through police lines or frozen zones designed to block recording. Any incident involving the press requires immediate DCPI notification. The patrol supervisor must conduct an immediate investigation if press members are assaulted or harassed.
Ensure subordinates understand that all grant/equipment/funding offers must be forwarded to the Police Commissioner — members have no authority to accept directly.
Preparation of a Traffic Intelligence Report does not relieve precinct personnel from taking other corrective action to expedite traffic flow and ensure pedestrian safety.
Patrol supervisor must respond personally when forced entry is detected at a DOE facility and assign a member to guard until the premises are secured.
Supervisors must verify coordinator availability before any auxiliary patrol deployment. No exceptions to the coordinator requirement.
Patrol supervisor shall respond and approve resolution (arrest, summons, court referral, or no action).
Patrol supervisor shall consult the duty captain when:

Patrol supervisor shall take command of the scene, coordinate with ESU, and ensure the area below is secured. Consider requesting additional resources (negotiators, mental health professionals) early.

Ensure desk officers are maintaining the Order of Protection Log properly and that service is attempted at least once per tour. The Domestic Violence Investigator is responsible for entering service information into the State Order of Protection Registry via FINEST.
Do NOT detain a properly identified and verified diplomat. All reports must still be prepared even when immunity applies.

The PDS/BRAM Supervisor is responsible for reviewing all burglary/robbery complaints to determine case status (ACTIVE or CLOSED) and ensuring all investigative steps have been taken. Major case prints must be taken for every burglary arrest.

Supervisors conduct the final review in the facial recognition process. Ensure all subordinates understand that a possible match is an investigative lead only and must never be presented as establishing probable cause.
Only authorized/approved protective vests will be worn.

Supervisors must ensure meal periods are properly scheduled and documented. Key responsibilities include: assigning meal periods prior to posting details, ensuring members are not deprived of meals, and properly documenting any exigent circumstances that prevent granting meals.
Desk officer shall check CPR System Roll Call to account for all members from previous tour. Conduct immediate investigation if any member failed to scan out.
The desk officer shall maintain the Interrupted Patrol Log under direct control at all times and review it frequently during the tour to ensure member presence is being expedited.

The supervisor shall review every Transit System Ejection Report for accuracy and completeness before approving it in FORMS. If the report is deficient, it must be returned to the member for correction.
During radio malfunction:

A sergeant can serve as Incident Commander at a Tier One response. However, any escalation beyond Tier One requires a higher-ranking IC. De-escalation is mandatory before enforcement -- this is not optional. Warnings must be given when feasible, and sufficient resources must be assembled before acting.

CAB protest liaisons may NOT wear civilian attire and may NOT be assigned to enforcement duties except in exceptional circumstances. If you see a CAB member in plainclothes at a demonstration, this is a procedure violation.

The arrest processing supervisor has direct responsibility for prisoner welfare: food, medical care, restrooms, and telephone access. Failure to provide these is a procedure violation. Track the clock -- 7 hours 30 minutes is the hard ceiling for First Amendment violation/misdemeanor processing.

While the full text is unavailable, know that at Tier One a sergeant can be the Incident Commander. Higher tiers require higher-ranking ICs. This tiered approach ensures proportional response to First Amendment events.
All supervisors must know the kit's location. If you are newly assigned to a command, learn the kit location immediately. Ensure inspections occur on schedule in January and July.

Be prepared for exam questions that test the order of alert levels or ask which color corresponds to which threat description. A common distractor is confusing BLUE (Guarded/General risk) with GREEN (Low risk).
A critique report with overtime reporting is required after every special event. The sergeant must ensure the detail roster is complete and accurate.
Do not assume someone else has made traffic notifications. Verify that TMC has been contacted, especially for unscheduled events where the Operations Unit must also be informed.
Know the three service methods: personal, door service, and deferred (CPO serves next business day). No NOTICE is served if a crime was actually committed at the premises.
Issuance is NOT mandatory. A repeated alarm cycle counts as one continuous action, not multiple violations. The white cardboard (agency) copy is affixed to the vehicle.

The critical distinction is vehicle vs. building: MOS may deactivate vehicle alarms but are NOT permitted to deactivate building alarms. For building alarms, a summons may be issued after 15 minutes of continuous sounding.
The supervisor must respond, investigate, and make the determination about whether the condition involves safety or is solely a civil penalty matter.
Determine the degree of hazard and decide whether barriers, posted officers, or ESU response is appropriate.
The patrol supervisor is the decision-maker on whether to secure the premises. The chain of custody for the key runs from ESU to the desk officer to the assigned member when the owner appears.
The supervisor's first action is to ask the owner/tenant their preference for repair. An MOS must be assigned to secure the location until repairs are complete.

The desk officer is responsible for reviewing the warrant for proper judicial signature and expiration. Police authority is strictly limited to preserving peace — DOB handles all enforcement and rescheduling.

The Special Ops Lt is responsible for packaging and forwarding information to the DA's Narcotics Eviction Program Coordinator. The CO/XO/Special Ops Lt must review all narcotics arrest reports for eviction potential.
Ensure MOS obtain the CVTF log number and enter it in the "Notifications" section of the Complaint Report Worksheet.

Refusal to attend nightlife meetings is NOT sufficient grounds for a C.U.R.E. operation. Know the three possible outcomes after the 30-day notice period: conduct operation, additional monitoring, or no further action.

Class A misdemeanor but NOT fingerprintable. If DV potential exists between family/household members, follow DV guidelines and do NOT demand entrance. Arrest only when the violator cannot be identified OR refuses to permit reentry. If complainant refuses to sign summons, UMOS may sign "based on information and belief."

The police role is exclusively peacekeeping. When arrest is made for unlawful eviction: OLBS only — no Complaint Report, no fingerprints, no photo, no DAT. If subject refuses entry to the marshal, the patrol supervisor should consider making them an EDP. Police cannot break in to let a tenant re-enter unless medicine vital to life is inside.

Special Ops Lt conducts a weekly inspection of the precinct mediation referral file. This program is reserved for situations where there is no basis to take enforcement action — it is not a substitute for proper enforcement.
The patrol supervisor is the decision-maker. Always consult Legal Bureau for obscenity matters. Know the distinction: PL 235 requires judicial determination; PL 245.11 allows summary enforcement.

The desk officer must notify the Dept of Health and Mental Hygiene by phone for three situations: human bitten/scratched/abraded, human exposed to rabid animal, or owner located after form forwarded. ESU bags the animal, RMP transports to ACC. Know all four dangerous dog categories and the Agriculture & Markets Law sections. ACC Brooklyn Control Center is open 24/7; Rescue Units are Mon-Fri 0800-2000.

Multiple supervisory layers with different frequencies: hourly (desk officer refresh), daily (0800 review, callbacks), weekly (chronic conditions list), monthly (311 caller audit). The 10-42 code is used to notify Communications Section with location and type of job. Disposition 10-95 for referrals to other agencies. The CO breaks the chain of command by personally directing the NCO Sgt. Give special attention to 311 assignments with media files (photos).
The activation mechanism is the FINEST message. For vehicles that can still move, the response is a warning and direction to leave, not immediate enforcement.

The desk officer must assign the 5-day follow-up check and then contact the Department of Sanitation if the vehicle remains. Derelict vehicles are only taken into police custody when needed as evidence.
The 2-month expiration threshold is the trigger. Know the distinction between unoccupied vehicles (remove plates on scene) and occupied vehicles (remove to stationhouse per P.G. 218-12).
Two key notifications: the owner about towing fees, and the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit about the vehicle. Highway vehicles can push to safe parking only — never to repair shops or private property.

Verify the desk officer has completed the full notification chain: ACS IRT, State Central Registry, SVU, and the appropriate borough Child Abuse Squad. Ensure the REPORT OF SUSPECTED CHILD ABUSE OR MALTREATMENT (PD377-154) is prepared.
A patrol supervisor MUST respond to ACS CPS assistance calls.
The desk officer must ensure MOS are trained in proper child car seat installation and use.
The training sergeant is responsible for ensuring ALL members are trained on child car seat usage.

Ensure the desk officer has verified that (1) the Juvenile Strategies Unit was contacted before arrest, (2) parent/guardian was notified immediately, (3) Miranda warnings were administered with parent/guardian present using PD244-1413, and (4) no questioning occurred if the parent objected or requested an attorney.
The CO must forward a report to the Chief of Department for specific premises types.
Ensure the desk officer completes the full notification chain — Missing Persons Squad in all cases, ACS if child unclaimed, and detective squad/SVU if abuse is suspected.
Verify that the desk officer has made all required notifications. For runaway children, Missing Persons Squad notification is mandatory regardless of whether the parent has been contacted.
Evaluate whether the offense justifies an on-campus arrest. For property crimes that are felonies, consider whether the arrest can be deferred to off-campus.
When responding to school incidents, ensure:

Ensure School Safety Agents understand that their search authority derives from the principal/designee, not from independent police authority. Monitor for compliance with the strip-search prohibition and same-gender search requirement.

The School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit must be consulted before taking enforcement action. The CO makes the final call on the threat level and response. Low-level threats should be handled through school discipline, not enforcement.

In death or likely-to-die cases, the desk officer will ensure a notification is made in a timely manner and document the time, date, and name of the member making notification on the AIDED REPORT or Police Accident Report.
Memorize all six categories. Note that Highway District members must be specifically assigned as collision technicians/investigators — not just any Highway member.

The patrol supervisor must confer with the ME or assistant ME prior to directing a member to search the body to safeguard property. The P/S also examines UMOS Digital A/L and certifies accuracy by signing rank and name, and makes their own Digital Activity Log entry per P.G. 216-04.

In cases where a death has occurred or the person is seriously injured and likely to die, the desk officer will ensure that notification is made in a timely manner and document the time, date, and name of the member making notification under caption "Notification" on the AIDED REPORT.
The patrol supervisor will immediately respond, ascertain facts, and attempt resolution. If unresolved, make a second request for an EMS supervisor.

The patrol supervisor decides whether to transport via RMP or wait for ambulance. Either way, destination is the hospital — never a police facility. The Sgt. can decide to use the RMP or await the bus per P.G. 216-01.
AED accountability happens every tour — the desk officer must account for all kits, assign them, notify dispatch, and make Command Log entries.
The patrol supervisor must confirm the Removal and Detention Order before proceeding. This is a legal document that authorizes the removal.
Patrol supervisors should be guided by P.G. 212-37, "Hazardous Materials," when responding to CO alarms and setting up frozen areas.
Patrol supervisors/integrity control officers shall comply with unloading procedures and make a visit entry in the Psychiatric Admitting Log when supervising subordinates.

It is City policy to pursue claims in its favor. Members must detail circumstances supporting City claims and obtain witness information. Full cooperation with the involved agency or Law Department is mandatory.
The supervisor at the Department facility must ensure the vehicle is not released prematurely — evidence preservation and defect correction are both gates to release.
The patrol supervisor has direct responsibility for supervising vehicle removal, summons issuance, and ensuring the IMPOUND screen is updated for towed vehicles.
The Quality of Life Officer reviews all Police Accident Reports — supervisors should verify this review is happening consistently.
The patrol supervisor initiates the Highway District request — this is the first step in the chemical test warrant process.
The patrol supervisor should weigh these six factors when deciding disposition. Intent and witness availability are the most critical factors favoring detective referral.
The supervising officer must ensure the unusual occurrence report includes the two additional copies for the Chief of Special Operations.
The patrol supervisor investigates and prepares the report for Department property damage — this is a direct supervisory responsibility.
Detective supervisors must ensure ECMS entry documenting date/time of delivery, Police Laboratory Number, and OCME Evidence Control Number for all DNA evidence.
Detective supervisors must ensure Biohazard Labels are properly utilized, review each INVOICE and REQUEST, and ensure ECMS entries documenting delivery are prepared.
Any discrepancy in property against INVOICE requires immediate IAB notification — this is a critical integrity safeguard.
The desk officer must verify identity, ensure proper documentation, and make Command Log entries for every temporary property removal.
CO should assign a supervisor to conduct the investigation.
COs must ensure adequate supply of pre-printed WORKSHEETS, INVOICES, and associated forms are stored in the property room for use during FORMS outages.
The Traffic Safety Sergeant has direct responsibility for coordinating timely vehicle delivery to Property Clerk Auto Pounds.
Commanding officers must ensure compliance is part of the command's monthly self-inspection program monitored by Quality Assurance Section.
CO must designate sufficient supervisors to frequently inspect perishable foods so items can be condemned and disposed of when necessary.
CO has final authority to approve or deny all Restricted Parking Permit applications.
CO must conduct a quarterly survey of generator/mechanical/electrical rooms to ensure proper use only.
Department reserves the right to review all computer activity — members have no privacy expectation on Department systems.
CO/designated supervisor must return Department smartphone to ITB when member has change in duty status (retired, suspended, modified).
The Operations Coordinator conducts the investigation into loss or theft of Department property.
CO must determine negligence and consult Department Advocate before initiating discipline for lost/stolen firearms.
Patrol supervisor vehicles carry additional emergency/tactical equipment. Ensure these items are present and serviceable each tour.
A Sergeant arriving first at a multi-agency incident may serve as the initial Command Element/Incident Commander until relieved through Transfer of Command by a higher-ranking officer.
An arriving sergeant may elect to make no change to the command element if the current officer is managing effectively — assuming command is not mandatory.

As a supervisor, you must respond to the scene when feasible and actively monitor the pursuit via radio. You have the authority and responsibility to direct termination if the danger to the public outweighs the benefit of apprehension.

Know the prohibited tactics — Forcible stopping and roadblocks are NEVER authorized during a vehicle pursuit. Officers may confuse these with permissible tactics like traffic control or PIM (which is only allowed on stationary vehicles).
Reckless Driving (VTL § 1212) is explicitly defined as a non-violent misdemeanor — pursuits for this offense alone are NOT authorized. This is heavily tested.
Force incident levels (1-4) do NOT apply to MOS injuries. Follow this procedure and A.G. 330-03 instead.

If EDP is contained and believed armed/violent but poses no immediate threat — take no additional action without CO/duty captain authorization. Establish firearms control immediately upon arrival. Use CIT-trained members as contact officers.

Patrol supervisor's priorities at firearms discharge: (1) call ambulance/render aid, (2) assume command, (3) establish crime scene, (4) inspect/secure firearms, (5) secure BWC, (6) notify desk officer, (7) request detective squad.
When a subject ingests narcotics, transport DIRECTLY to nearest hospital — do NOT use force to prevent swallowing or attempt to remove from mouth.

Supervisor must assume command, coordinate de-escalation, and direct tactics to minimize injury. Must consider whether non-compliance is deliberate resistance or an inability to comply (medical condition, mental impairment, language barrier, etc.).

For Level 1 incidents, the immediate supervisor is responsible for all investigative steps. For Level 2-4, the immediate supervisor is guided by the investigating supervisor. Complete Level 1 T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT before end of tour.
Ensure CEWs are accounted for at start of every tour with Command Log entry including serial numbers. Ensure function test is performed into firearms safety station under your observation.

Know the three types of prohibited statements: (1) False — intentional untruth, (2) Misleading — intended to misdirect/omit material facts, (3) Inaccurate — grossly negligent incorrect information. The discipline differs — false statements result in separation absent extraordinary circumstances.
Extraordinary circumstances for avoiding separation on false statement charges can only be determined by the Police Commissioner on a case-by-case basis. This is heavily tested.
Members must review documents before signing. Claiming "I didn't read it" is not a defense to an inaccurate statement charge.

Supervisory responsibilities are never abrogated — ranking officers must take affirmative action when observing corruption or misconduct, including immediate IAB notification. This applies whether on or off duty, inside or outside NYC.
Know the uniform permission hierarchy: (1) Outside NYC = Police Commissioner/First Deputy/Chief of Department, (2) Social events = Commanding Officer or designee.

Out-of-state firearms require permission from Police Commissioner/First Deputy/Chief of Department even if the state allows out-of-jurisdiction officers to carry. State law does not override Department policy.
Members must notify commanding officer PRIOR to acceptance — not after. Pre-approval is required.
Know the two-track process: (1) Reward for police service = Police Commissioner decides, (2) Gift from city business dealer = Commanding Officer decides after reviewing City Charter § 2604(B)(5).
Two or more gifts in a calendar year = report to Deputy Commissioner, Internal Affairs regardless of value or source.

Commanding Officers must conduct semi-annual DMV inquiries and warrant checks for all UMOS and CMOS. When IAB flags a license issue, the member is immediately prohibited from driving Department vehicles until the matter is resolved.
Ensure members are informed of this policy. Personal use and dissemination of derogatory/discriminatory material are strictly prohibited and subject to disciplinary action.

As a supervisor, return salutes promptly. Ensure subordinates understand salute protocols, especially the distinction between saluting the desk vs. the Desk Officer, and the different protocols for Captain vs. Deputy Inspector rank thresholds.

Supervisors should be familiar with HELPLINE, POPPA, and Counseling Services Unit referral pathways. Early detection and referral is the Department's objective. Participation in counseling does NOT jeopardize promotions. Counseling is confidential and NOT part of the disciplinary system.

Know the distinction between the head covering and facial hair accommodation processes. Both use the same form and apply to the same groups, but the ID update mechanisms differ — head coverings go through the Shield/ID Unit for a new ID card, while facial hair goes into RAPNET.

Supervisors must be vigilant about prohibited conduct, particularly unauthorized relationships with subordinates, CIs, witnesses, victims, and youth. The political restrictions and Community Board rules are frequently tested areas.

Know the complete breast bar hierarchy from top (US Flag) to bottom (Firearms Proficiency). Verify members wear bars in correct order during inspections. Watch for unauthorized cloth flags or fraternal pins — only the American Flag breast bar is authorized at the top position. (A.G. 305-13)

Verify holster compliance (retention device, trigger guard coverage, no clip-on/belly band). Ensure members have passed qualification before carrying modified firearms. Know the Family Court/Supreme Court unarmed requirement.

If member is incapacitated, the supervisory officer must sign and submit the report within 30 days. Verify that damaged article is presented unrepaired and that the member was performing police duty (or extraordinary police action for civilian clothing).

Ensure members of the command wear mourning bands during the correct time periods. For LOD deaths, verify compliance through the 10th day after death for command members. Know that the flag period and mourning band period have different reference dates (funeral vs death). (A.G. 305-14)

As a supervisor, inspect vests when serviceability concerns are raised. Know the approval chain for not wearing: Bureau Chief/Deputy Commissioner for uniform, Immediate Supervisor for civilian enforcement. Direct members to the Patrol Borough for reserve supply regardless of their assigned bureau. Ensure members who are shot while wearing a vest — even if apparently uninjured — visit a doctor. (A.G. 305-15)

As a supervisor, ensure compliance with unauthorized adornment prohibitions during roll call and throughout the tour. Verify belt equipment order at annual pistol qualification. Confirm civilian clothes members carry the Color of the Day band. (A.G. 305-08)

Know the 8 items the interrogating officer must inform the member about. No "off the record" questions, threats, or reward promises. Interrogation must be recorded. Post-charge interrogation must be completed 10 days before trial. Assign member to 2nd Platoon.

The first supervisor on scene must (1) direct member to remain, (2) prepare PD469-150 immediately, (3) notify CO/XO/duty captain. Supervisory officer is mandated to contact Counseling Services Unit. IAB Command Center must be notified for log number.

Know the 5 forms required: WCS-110 (Employee Statement), WCS-100 (Supervisor Report of Injury), WCS-120 (Witness Statement), DP2002 (Employee Option), and Time Usage 49. Chief of Personnel grants leave with pay for first week.

As a supervisor, you are responsible for ensuring personal folders are properly maintained, documents are filed correctly, and purge schedules are followed. When members transfer out, verify the sealed confidential envelope and photocopy of Force Record are provided. Check NYS driver's license on assignment.

While sergeants are not typically the primary grant administrators, you may serve as programmatic or fiscal contacts. Know the key rules: grant funds cannot supplant existing funding, overtime-in-time does not expend grant funds, equipment bidding takes 12-18 months, and HUD grants go through Housing Bureau. Timely reporting and compliance are your direct responsibility as a command contact.
Know the property return matrix. Tier 2 mandatory retirement age is 63; Tier 3 is 62. Members on terminal leave are only available for emergencies. No leaves after terminal leave starts.

Coordinate documentation of all involved MOS names when numerous individuals are exposed. Ensure exposure report number obtained via LOD Injury Reporting System. If actual injury → comply with A.G. 330-03. Know the bleach ratio and needle handling rules for roll call training.

Sign off LOD Injury Report electronically before end of tour. Include BWC/video review findings. If member dies or likely to die, ensure firearms and Department property are safeguarded. Comply with P.G. 221-06 if any force was used. FECA indicator required on LOD report.

Ensure involved members are available for Trauma Team interview. Verify members have been contacted in person. Prepare UOR listing contacted members. Captain or above can request Trauma Team response to any incident.

The first sixteen months of a probationary police officer's field assignment is a critical phase. You must conscientiously evaluate probationary officers and use the SUPERVISOR FEEDBACK FORM, command discipline, and charges to document below-standards performance. Don't wait for scheduled evaluation periods if problems arise — submit interim evaluations immediately.

The first fifteen months of an investigative assignment is a critical phase. If there is a change in performance after the fifteenth month, you MUST immediately submit an interim evaluation. For "Chronic Absent - Category B" investigators, you bear the burden of justifying any rating above "Low" and must explicitly state whether the ratee should remain in the investigative assignment.

As a commanding officer, you are directly responsible for auditing investigative time, ensuring the 8th and 15th month evaluations are prepared and forwarded, and guaranteeing all PERFORMANCE EVALUATIONS are finalized. Failure to complete evaluations on time is a common accountability issue on the exam.

You are responsible for monitoring and documenting subordinates' performance throughout the rating period — the PERF System does not relieve you of this continuous responsibility. Ensure quarterly evaluations are completed before starting annual evaluations, and be aware of the January 31st finalization deadline.

Raters must conscientiously evaluate probationary sergeants and lieutenants to ensure members unable to satisfactorily perform their duties are identified. Interim evaluations must be submitted when necessary (e.g., change in the ratee's performance). Do not wait for scheduled evaluation periods if performance issues arise.

As a rater, you are responsible for applying the military service evaluation rules correctly. Key details: (1) average of 3 prior evaluations, (2) floor is the higher of satisfactory (3.0) or the last pre-absence rating, (3) mandatory notation in Rater's Comments citing NYS Military Law and duration of military duty. Direct questions to Performance Analysis Unit or Legal Bureau.

While this procedure covers captains and above, know the MBO system (5 KRAs) and the 4th/10th/16th month probation timeline. Also know the military service formula (average of 3 prior, floor of 3.0 or last rating) — it applies across all evaluation procedures.

As a rater, you must inform the civilian ratee of the tasks and standards at the beginning of the evaluation period — not mid-period or after the fact. Failure to do so can invalidate the evaluation on appeal.

As a reviewer (typically a sergeant or lieutenant), you must examine and sign the evaluation form, forward a copy to the commanding officer, and ensure the remaining copy is filed in the ratee's personal folder at the command. Do not skip the filing step — it is part of the official record.

As a supervisor of civilian personnel, you are responsible for initiating and maintaining the supervisory conference and warning documentation that supports below-standards ratings. Without proper documentation, the rating may be overturned on appeal. File CIVILIAN RECORD OF SUPERVISORY CONFERENCES and CIVILIAN LATENESS/WARNING NOTIFICATIONS promptly as issues arise — do not wait until the evaluation period ends.
Know the routing distinction — members below captain route appeals to the Personnel Officer concerned, while captains and above route to the Personnel Officer, Chief of Personnel.
As a sergeant, you may serve as a rater whose evaluation is being appealed. Ensure evaluations are based solely on performance factors and are factually accurate to withstand appeal scrutiny.

As a rater, your evaluation of civilian members can be appealed. Ensure your evaluations are fact-based and free from bias. Know the four valid grounds for appeal — if a civilian member claims one of these, the appeal process must be followed. The 30-day timeline for scheduling the interview and for the review board to resolve the appeal are hard deadlines.

As a supervisor, if a member comes to you about a transgender or gender non-conforming transition, your role is to treat the matter with sensitivity and confidentiality, facilitate the meeting with the CO or OEEO, and ensure the member's preferred name and pronouns are respected. Do NOT disclose the member's status to others without their consent — the member decides when, with whom, and how much to share.

If a member reports sick on a testing day, they must visit a Department surgeon — a regular doctor's note is not sufficient. Only a Department surgeon can approve excusal from the test. Members on court or training must report for testing immediately after completion.

As a supervisor, know the full chain of consequences for positive drug tests: Charges and Specifications plus suspension. If a member refuses to submit to random drug screening, they must be suspended and the refusal is grounds for dismissal. Members on sick report, regular days off, military leave, annual vacation, terminal leave, or bereavement leave are excused — all other members MUST report.

If a member refuses drug screening for a specialized unit assignment, the member's permanent command suspends them and follows A.G. 318-06. Refusal equals suspension and grounds for dismissal — this is consistent across all drug screening procedures.

Passing a drug screening test for a specialized unit assignment does NOT exempt a member from random drug screening. Members who pass are still subject to the Department's random drug screening program under A.G. 332-05 or A.G. 332-08, depending on their assignment.

Under A.G. 332-08, excusal for individual days off requires approval from the bureau chief/deputy commissioner (not just bureau chief as in A.G. 332-05). This is because IAB reports to a Deputy Commissioner, so the approval authority includes both bureau chief and deputy commissioner.
Key comparison of the three drug screening procedures:

A candidate for civil service promotion can withdraw with no penalty at any time before they receive notification to report for drug screening. Once notified to report, withdrawal is no longer an option. The written request goes to the commanding officer, who forwards two copies to the Chief of Personnel.

As a supervisor, understand the full chain of consequences for positive drug tests: Charges and Specifications plus suspension. Know that the member has 60 days to request independent testing at their own expense, through the Department Advocate's Office.

Note that for discretionary promotions, the restoration is to the member's "prior title" (not "prior civil service title" as in A.G. 332-09). This is because discretionary promotions involve ranks that may not be civil service positions.
The key differences between A.G. 332-09 (civil service) and A.G. 332-10 (discretionary) drug screening:

As a supervisor, if a member approaches you about voluntary drug testing, be aware of the 24-hour time limit and that the request goes to the Chief of Personnel. The member is entitled to consult with an attorney or union rep before making the request. The screening covers both hair and urine — not just one type.
Even if a member passes voluntary drug testing, they remain subject to the Department's random drug screening program. Voluntary testing does not create any exemption from future random screening.

The Medical Division performs the voluntary drug screening only after the Chief of Personnel has verified the allegation and the 24-hour time requirement. The screening itself covers both hair and urine — this is mandatory for voluntary testing, unlike other procedures where only one type may be collected.

As a supervisor, you are responsible for ensuring members scheduled for screening actually report. Know the three valid exceptions (sick, bereavement, terminal leave). Members on court must report after court or be rescheduled — court is not an excuse to skip testing. Monitor members returning from the Counseling Services Unit Program to ensure they complete the required screening.

As a supervisor, ensure probationary officers under your command are scheduled for end of probation medical exams within the six-month window. If a probationary officer reports sick on the testing date, they must visit a Department surgeon and obtain approval for excusal. Be aware that the same-sex observer requirement applies to both urine and hair collection for chain of custody purposes. Understanding the distinction between this procedure and random drug screening (A.G. 332-11) is critical — probationary officers are subject to both.

As a supervisor, if a member reports retaliation, you must understand the distinction: misconduct/corruption retaliation is investigated by IAB; EEO-related retaliation is investigated by OEEO. Direct the member accordingly.

Know the available resources for members experiencing retaliation or related stress. As a supervisor, you should be prepared to direct members to the Employee Assistance Unit, Chaplains Unit, or POPPA. This is both a leadership and exam topic.

At every step of the grievance procedure, the grievant and the commanding officer considering the grievance shall work for a satisfactory adjustment. The supervisor shall have the right to summon any persons considered necessary for an equitable adjustment. Proceedings will be informal.

Know the Personnel Grievance Board composition: C.O. Office of Labor Relations (Chair), Chief of Department or designee, Chief of Personnel or designee, plus the grievant's union presidents or designee. The Board must convene at least once per month.

As a sergeant, you may be the commanding officer at Step I. You must respond within 5 days, consider the grievance objectively, and work toward a satisfactory adjustment informally. Remember: grievances do not include disciplinary matters. The grievant has the right to an employee representative. Failing to follow lawful orders is never justified by the grievance process.

As a commanding officer, you must endorse all official trip requests with a clear APPROVAL or DISAPPROVAL. If approved, ensure all required documents are forwarded through the proper chain of command without delay.

Ensure members under your command submit their expense reports within the ten-day deadline. The Director, Audits and Accounts Unit will initiate measures to ensure compliance if reports are not submitted in a timely manner.

Know the full approval chain for official trips: Member prepares request and forms, Commanding Officer endorses, Highest Ranking Member concerned forwards to First Deputy Commissioner, First Deputy Commissioner recommends to Police Commissioner, Office of Police Commissioner forwards to Mayor. Track timelines carefully -- twenty business days domestic, thirty days international.

As a supervisor, you must verify and sign the monthly Labor Management Joint Activity Report, ensure no overtime/comp/meal is granted for release time, and forward reports to the Office of Labor Relations by the 10th of each month. Members who fail to submit proper reports may face de-certification and loss of pay.

If a member under your supervision fails to qualify, you must ensure the ACIU is notified immediately and the PD124-141 score sheet is faxed. Monitor the 90-day timeline closely — if the member does not qualify, charges must be prepared. Know that Section 75 hearings can result in removal from the Department.

The integrity control officer must redact identifying information before the member views the file. This protects witness and complainant identities while still allowing the member to review their own records. Ensure this step is completed before any review takes place.

The commanding officer has a dual role: if the preliminary investigation shows the entry is appropriate, the CO informs the member; if the appeal is verified, the CO endorses it through channels. Know both outcomes and the corresponding actions.

As a supervisor, you should be aware that members have the right to review their CPI file annually. If a member raises concerns about entries in their file, guide them through the proper appeal process — written appeal on Typed Letterhead to the commanding officer. Do not attempt to resolve CPI disputes informally.

As a supervisor, you are responsible for monitoring whether your subordinates have reached their FLSA rolling cap. Once the cap is hit, all further overtime must be paid in cash — you cannot continue accruing comp time beyond the cap.

You must enforce overtime authorization requirements. Failure to do so can result in unpaid overtime claims against the Department and disciplinary action against the supervisor who authorized or permitted the unauthorized overtime.

These four considerations are direct supervisory responsibilities. Pay special attention to item (b) — employees may not work during meal periods without written authorization. If you allow it without authorization, you may be creating an unpaid overtime liability. Item (c) makes record keeping "absolutely mandatory" — the language is intentionally strong.

You must ensure that each covered employee under your command records their starting and finishing times. Failure to maintain accurate time records creates liability for unpaid overtime claims and is a direct supervisory failure.

As a supervisor, you must grant preliminary approval of a breast milk accommodation request pending OEEO's final written determination. Do not deny or delay the request — preliminary approval must be granted while awaiting OEEO's review.

As a supervisor, you must grant preliminary approval for prayer accommodation requests pending OEEO determination. You must also confer with the requesting member about the length, time, and location of prayer, and inform them of any designated prayer space.

As a supervisor, you have specific responsibilities in the reasonable accommodation process: (1) notify the commanding officer, (2) grant preliminary approval pending OEEO determination, (3) confer with the requesting member, (4) confer with operations coordinator if needed, and (5) assist with locating appropriate space. Failure to grant preliminary approval or retaliating against a requesting member carries disciplinary consequences.

Executive members (captain and above) who wish to participate must be approved by the Chief of Personnel prior to participation. Ensure members in your command understand all eligibility requirements before they apply.

As a supervisor, you may be asked to confer with the commanding officer regarding a member's eligibility. Be prepared to provide input on the member's duty status, attendance record, and any monitoring or Chronic Sick designation.

Sergeants should be aware of Paid Detail assignments within their command. Monitoring Paid Detail activities against roll call and overtime records helps identify potential conflicts, fatigue issues, or policy violations. The command overtime self-inspection program is a key supervisory tool.

As a patrol supervisor, you may be called to the scene of a Paid Detail arrest. You must determine whether the Paid Detail member will process the arrest or if on-duty personnel will handle it. Paid Detail members generally do not process arrests unless directed by an on-duty NYPD supervisor.
As a supervisor (CO, XO, or ICO), you are responsible for signing the white copy of the Paid Detail Card. Verify that entries are accurate before signing.

Under no circumstances may members switch a Paid Detail assignment or perform a Paid Detail assignment scheduled by another member. A Paid Detail assignment must be performed by the member who scheduled the assignment. Failure to comply with this directive will result in immediate and indefinite suspension from the Paid Detail Program.

As a supervisor, be vigilant about monitoring Paid Detail compliance. Key areas to check: (1) members working within prohibited time windows, (2) failure to make Activity Log entries, (3) failure to sign Paid Detail Cards properly, (4) members exceeding hour limits, and (5) improper cancellations.
